Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?

askNext: What exact meaning of Image API is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
image-api.body: Image API belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.
image-api.example: "The team said image api as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
image-api.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
image-api.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

image-api.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.
image-api.whyExists: It exists because conversations around GPT Image 2 need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.

askNext: What exact meaning of Image generation is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
image-generation.body: Image generation earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
image-generation.example: "Image generation came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
image-generation.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
image-generation.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

image-generation.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
image-generation.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Midjourney often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: What exact meaning of Industrial policy is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
industrial-policy.body: Industrial policy belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.
industrial-policy.example: "The industrial policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
industrial-policy.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
industrial-policy.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

industrial-policy.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
industrial-policy.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Sovereign AI are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: What model is doing the inference, where is it running, and who pays per call?
Is the bottleneck latency, throughput, quality, or context assembly?
Can some of this workload be cached, batched, or offloaded to a smaller model?
inference.body: If someone says a feature is "AI-powered", inference is usually the operational question hiding underneath. Not whether the model is clever in principle, but whether the system can answer quickly, cheaply, and often enough to survive contact with users.
inference.example: "Training happened once in the background; inference happens every time a customer opens the feature and expects an answer in under two seconds."
inference.misuse: People often use "inference" as a grand-sounding synonym for "the AI bit". That hides the practical questions. Inference is not magic reasoning dust. It is a serving workload with latency, throughput, reliability, and cost characteristics.
inference.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means API calls, GPU time, queueing, prompt assembly, caching, streaming, rate limits, and a finance person eventually asking why the bot became expensive after launch. If your AI feature is live, inference is the meter running.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The part customers can actually feel.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A performance budget attached to compute, prompts, and product expectations.

inference.whyExists: The term distinguishes using a model from building one. Training changes weights. Inference uses those weights to do work on fresh data. The distinction matters because the technical constraints, costs, and risks are very different.

askNext: What part of the margin comes from routing skill, and what part from customer convenience?
How quickly does the advantage disappear if providers cut prices or copy the feature?
Is there product substance here, or mainly a spread between wholesale tokens and retail confidence?
inference-arbitrage.body: Arbitrage is not shameful. It is merely less mythic than many decks would prefer.
inference-arbitrage.example: "Their moat turned out to be inference arbitrage with a charming UI and a very aggressive newsletter."
inference-arbitrage.misuse: The term is stretched when any wrapper, gateway, or comparison site is described as though routing alone were a durable moat. Arbitrage can be real. It is also famously allergic to permanence.
inference-arbitrage.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means mixing models behind the scenes, swapping suppliers when pricing shifts, and capturing some spread through UX, bundling, reliability, or customer ignorance. The better businesses add real workflow value. The thinner ones are mostly tariffs with branding.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Capture value from differences between model suppliers instead of betting on a single one.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is shopping the model market so the customer can pretend not to notice.

inference-arbitrage.whyExists: Model markets are uneven. Providers price differently, regions behave differently, and many users would rather pay one intermediary than personally compare latency tables like a day trader with a prompt addiction.

askNext: What is the maximum acceptable cost for this user action?
Which requests can use a cheaper model, cache, or shorter reasoning path?
Are retries and tool calls counted in the same budget as the visible model response?
inference-budget.body: Inference budgets are not anti-intelligence. They are the moment the system admits that cleverness has a marginal cost.
inference-budget.example: "The inference budget survived launch only after the agent was stopped from spending premium reasoning on password reset emails."
inference-budget.misuse: It gets reduced to token counting. Tokens matter, but so do model choice, retries, tool calls, reasoning effort, cache hits, latency targets, and the number of times a workflow politely tries again.
inference-budget.practicalMeaning: In practice it means per-user limits, per-task ceilings, model routing policies, cache strategy, timeout rules, alerting, and deciding which requests deserve the expensive model before the expensive model decides for itself.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: Set a ceiling on how much intelligence a workflow is allowed to buy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A practical argument between quality, speed, and the bill.

inference-budget.whyExists: Model calls have variable cost and performance characteristics. Without a budget, a successful feature can become a financial incident, and an agent can discover new forms of curiosity at the company's expense.

askNext: Who actually trains the model, and who merely serves it?
What happens to cost and performance when traffic scales?
Which data-handling guarantees survive the extra layer?
inference-provider.body: Inference providers matter because very few teams want to run model serving properly, and a surprising number still want to sound as if they do.
inference-provider.example: "They said they had their own AI platform, which turned out to mean an inference provider, a neat dashboard, and a heroic willingness to rename dependency as strategy."
inference-provider.misuse: By blurring the line between building a model and hosting one, or by describing a rented endpoint as proprietary intelligence simply because the invoice arrives under a different logo.
inference-provider.practicalMeaning: In practice the choice of inference provider shapes latency, price, regional availability, data handling, model access, and how much of your application's reliability now depends on somebody else's serving layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is handling the GPU headaches, and now they sit in your critical path.

inference-provider.whyExists: Because reliable model serving requires GPUs, batching, scaling, observability, routing, and a tolerance for operational pain that many product teams would rather outsource.

askNext: Which parts of the stack protect reliability, and which merely create another place for failure to hide?
How are routing, caching, and fallback policies affecting quality as well as cost?
Who can explain the per-request economics without opening six dashboards and a prayer?
inference-stack.body: Once the prototypes leave the demo phase, the stack arrives carrying invoices, dashboards, and opinions.
inference-stack.example: "The inference stack matured rapidly once finance started reading the token invoices with feeling."
inference-stack.misuse: By calling every thin wrapper a stack. A real inference stack handles production constraints. A dropdown of model names and a vibes-based retry loop do not amount to infrastructure.
inference-stack.practicalMeaning: In practice it means SDKs, proxies, model routers, rate limits, prompt templates, redaction, logging, eval pipelines, cache policies, billing dashboards, and repeated arguments over whether quality improved enough to justify the spend.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Everything around the model call that keeps production from becoming an expensive hobby.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered serving system where cost, latency, safety, and uptime argue with one another on every request.

inference-stack.whyExists: Because shipping AI features requires more than model access. Real applications need reliability, latency control, policy enforcement, failover, measurement, and a defensible explanation for the monthly bill.
